transcript: The key technique is to use the 'pre-commit hook' pattern: before every commit, run eslint --fix and prettier. This catches 80% of style issues automatically.
Use the Eisenhower Matrix: urgent vs important. Put tasks into 4 quadrants and only work on quadrant 1 and 2. This single framework increased my output by 3x.
When debugging, always reproduce the bug with the smallest possible input. This is called 'minimal reproduction'. It reduces debugging time from hours to minutes.
The Pomodoro technique: 25 minutes focused work + 5 minutes break. After 4 cycles, take a 15-30 minute break. This is proven to maintain dopamine levels.
Use 'rubber duck debugging': explain your code line by line to a rubber duck (or any object). The act of verbalizing forces you to slow down and find the bug.
The '2-minute rule' from GTD: if a task takes less than 2 minutes, do it immediately. This prevents small tasks from piling up into overwhelming lists.
When learning a new framework, build the 'todo app' first. It forces you to implement CRUD, state management, and routing — the three core patterns.
Use 'inversion thinking': instead of asking 'how do I succeed?', ask 'what would cause me to fail?' Then systematically eliminate those causes.
The '80/20 rule' applied to learning: identify the 20% of concepts that explain 80% of usage. Master those first before going deeper.
Always write the test before the code (TDD). It forces you to define the interface and expected behavior first, resulting in cleaner, more testable code.
